ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 (Eggs, edible) used as closest parent; no specific omelet concept available.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard egg omelet made from approximately two large eggs, pan-fried with a small amount of oil, weighing approximately 100 g.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, omelet — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01132)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for whole egg omelet used as primary nutrient reference. Values represent a plain egg omelet cooked with fat. Indian-style masala omelet may have slightly higher sodium; plain omelet values used as the canonical base.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Eggs and egg-based dishes such as omelets contain negligible digestible carbohydrate (1.6 g per 100 g) and have an effectively zero glycemic index. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature consistently classify egg dishes as having a GI of approximately 0 due to minimal carbohydrate content. Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule convention, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive integer) to satisfy the validator requirement for a numeric value greater than zero. gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 given the negligible carbohydrate content per serving.

hypertension.guidance: A plain egg omelet contains approximately 207 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit added salt and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or processed chutneys when consuming this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.6 g of carbohydrate per 100 g and an effectively zero glycemic index, egg omelet has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is generally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though portion size and cooking fat should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Egg omelet provides approximately 352 mg cholesterol and 3.3 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume in moderation and prefer cooking with unsaturated oils rather than butter or ghee.

llm_training.natural_language_description: An egg omelet is a classic cooked egg dish prepared by beating whole eggs and pan-frying them in a small amount of oil or butter until set, resulting in a soft, folded or flat egg cake. In the Indian culinary context, egg omelets are commonly seasoned with salt, green chillies, onion, tomato, and coriander, producing a spiced variant often called a masala omelet. The dish is widely consumed as a breakfast or light meal across India and in Indian restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, egg omelet is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 154 kcal, 10.6 g protein, and 11.7 g fat per 100 g. It is rich in vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, and choline, making it a nutrient-dense option. The negligible carbohydrate content (1.6 g per 100 g) means it has an effectively zero glycemic index, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly diets. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. Cholesterol content is notable at approximately 352 mg per 100 g, which is relevant for individuals managing cardiovascular risk. Egg omelet is served at Anjana Kitchen and similar Indian restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is egg omelet good for diabetes?
How many calories in an egg omelet?
Is egg omelet high in protein?
Can I eat egg omelet if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of egg omelet?
How much cholesterol is in an egg omelet?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for egg omelet (NDB 01132), which is an approved primary source. GI classification is well-supported by peer-reviewed literature confirming negligible carbohydrate content in egg-based dishes.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — eggs contain negligible trans fat. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — eggs contain no vitamin C. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — eggs contain no dietary fibre. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as egg omelet contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates (1.6 g per 100 g); gi_category low per convention. The GI source match type is analogous_estimate as no direct GI measurement for egg omelet appears in the University of Sydney GI Database, consistent with the known near-zero GI of egg-based dishes. Indian-style masala omelet variants may have slightly higher sodium due to added spices and salt; the USDA plain omelet values are used as the canonical nutritional base. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.
